Week 2 - Preprocessing, part 2¶

1. Lesson: None¶

2. Weekly graph question¶

The Storytelling With Data book mentions planning on a "Who, What, and How" for your data story. Write down a possible Who, What, and How for your data, using the ideas in the book.

  • Who: - Students and their families
  • What:- Analyze a dataset containing information about student performance relative to other features
  • How: -

3. Homework - work with your own data¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
In [2]:
df = pd.read_csv('..\SID.tsv',sep='\t', engine='python')
data5 = pd.read_csv('..\data5.csv', sep=',', engine='python')
dcd25 = pd.read_csv('..\DCD_2025.tsv', sep='\t', engine='python')

CHECKLIST¶

This week, you will do the same types of exercises as last week, but you should use your chosen datasets that someone in your class found last semester. (They likely will not be the particular datasets that you found yourself.)

Here are some types of analysis you can do Use Google, documentation, and ChatGPT to help you:¶

  • Summarize the datasets using info() and describe()

  • Are there any duplicate rows?

  • Are there any duplicate values in a given column (when this would be inappropriate?)

  • What are the mean, median, and mode of each column?

  • Are there any missing or null values?

    • Do you want to fill in the missing value with a mean value? A value of your choice? Remove that row?

  • Identify any other inconsistent data (e.g. someone seems to be taking an action before they are born.)

  • Encode any categorical variables (e.g. with one-hot encoding.)

Conclusions:¶

  • Are the data usable? If not, find some new data!

  • Do you need to modify or correct the data in some way?

  • Is there any class imbalance? (Categories that have many more items than other categories).

summarize the data¶

function to find missing values percent in each column¶

In [15]:
def find_missing(df):
    missing_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Column': df.columns,
    'Missing_Count': df.isnull().sum().values,
    'Missing_Percent': (df.isnull().sum() / len(df) * 100).values
    })
    missing_summary = missing_summary.sort_values('Missing_Percent', ascending=False)
    print(missing_summary)
    
    return missing_summary

check for columns missing a given percentage of values¶

Function to check columns¶
In [19]:
# Show columns with missing values between lower and upper bounds and include the number of columns

def show_missing_columns(df, lower_bound, upper_bound):
    missing_percent = (df.isnull().sum() / len(df)) * 100
    filtered_missing = missing_percent[(missing_percent > lower_bound) & (missing_percent <= upper_bound)]
    count = len(filtered_missing)
    
    # Generate markdown table
    table_df = filtered_missing.reset_index()
    table_df.columns = ['Column', 'Missing %']
    table_df['Missing %'] = table_df['Missing %'].round(2)
    print(f"Count: \033[1m{count}\033[0m columns with missing values between {lower_bound}% and {upper_bound}%:")
    print(table_df.to_markdown(index=False))
    print('\n')
    
    return filtered_missing, count
Calls to function¶
In [20]:

df_Percent80_95, df_percent80_95_count = show_missing_columns(df, 80, 95)
df_Percent70_80, df_percent70_80_count = show_missing_columns(df, 70, 80)
df_Percent50_70, df_percent50_70_count = show_missing_columns(df, 50, 70)

Information Printout for missing columns¶
In [24]:
unneeded_cols = ['stabbr', 'state_name']
replace_cols = ['region4', 'region9']

print(
    "1. Dropping the state abbreviation and the state name is inconsequential since\n"
    "   the state FIPS code uniquely identifies each state.\n"
    f"2. Drop columns that could be replaced: \033[1m\033[4m{unneeded_cols}\033[3m\033[0m\n"
    f"3. Consider replacing region columns with numeric data later:  \033[1m\033[4m{replace_cols}\033[3m\033[0m\n"
    )
print(f"Columns with more than 95% missing values: \033[1m\033[4m{df_percent95_count}\033[3m\033[0m,\n",df_Percent95.index.tolist())
print(f"Columns with 80-95% missing values: \033[1m\033[4m{df_percent80_95_count}\033[3m\033[0m,\n",df_Percent80_95.index.tolist())
print(f"Columns with 70-80% missing values: \033[1m\033[4m{df_percent70_80_count}\033[3m\033[0m,\n",df_Percent70_80.index.tolist())
print(f"Columns with 50-70% missing values: \033[1m\033[4m{df_percent50_70_count}\033[3m\033[0m,\n",df_Percent50_70.index.tolist())

1. Dropping the state abbreviation and the state name is inconsequential since
   the state FIPS code uniquely identifies each state.
2. Drop columns that could be replaced: ['stabbr', 'state_name']
3. Consider replacing region columns with numeric data later:  ['region4', 'region9']

South        510
West         390
dtype: int64
In [35]:
# ######################################################################################
# #####     NOTE: Running this cell by itself more than once will result in the    ##### 
# #####     region4 and region9 columns being replaced multiple times,             ##### 
# #####     ending with missing values                                             ##### 
# ######################################################################################
# put code for replacing region4 and region9 here
# step 1 - make a copy of the dataframe after dropping unneeded columns
df_copy_after_95drops = data_with_drops95.copy()
# step 2 - replace region4 and region9 with numerical values
region4_mapping = {
    'Northeast': 1,
    'Midwest': 2,
    'South': 3,
    'West': 4
}
df_copy_after_95drops['region4'] = df_copy_after_95drops['region4'].map(region4_mapping)
region9_mapping = {
    'East North Central': 1,
    'East South Central': 2,
    'Middle Atlantic': 3,
    'Mountain': 4,    
    'New England': 5,
    'Pacific': 6,
    'South Atlantic': 7,
    'West South Central': 8,
    'West North Central': 9,
}
df_copy_after_95drops['region9'] = df_copy_after_95drops['region9'].map(region9_mapping)
# copy the changed dataframe back to data_with_drops95
data_with_drops95 = df_copy_after_95drops
# copy changed dataframe back to new
df_new = data_with_drops95
# check new dataframe
df_new

df dataframe¶
fill missing values with mode when feature is numeric¶
In [39]:
# Fill missing values in numeric columns with their mode
dfnew = data_with_drops95.copy()
numeric_cols = dfnew.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns
dfnew[numeric_cols] = dfnew[numeric_cols].fillna(dfnew[numeric_cols].mode().iloc[0])

# Verify the changes
print("Missing values after filling with mode:")
print(dfnew.isnull().sum())

fill missing values in categorical columns¶
In [44]:
# Fill missing values in categorical (object) columns with 'NONE'
cat_cols = data_with_drops95.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'category']).columns
data_with_drops95[cat_cols] = data_with_drops95[cat_cols].fillna('NONE')

# Verify the changes
print("Missing values after filling categorical columns with 'NONE':")
print(data_with_drops95[cat_cols].isnull().sum())
data_with_drops95 # check rows/instances and columns/features

df dataframe¶
Create Correlation matrices of numeric columns using a copy of the dataframe¶
In [45]:
numeric_columns = data_with_drops95.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns.tolist()
mydf_numeric = data_with_drops95[numeric_columns]

correlation_matrix = mydf_numeric.corr()
print("Correlation Matrix of Numeric Values:")
print(correlation_matrix)

# Visualize the correlation matrix using matplotlib
plt.figure(figsize=(90, 70))
cmap = plt.cm.RdBu_r

# Plot the heatmap
im = plt.imshow(correlation_matrix, cmap=cmap, vmin=-1, vmax=1)
plt.colorbar(im)

plt.title('State Indicators Dataset Correlation Matrix', fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(np.arange(len(numeric_columns)), numeric_columns, rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.yticks(np.arange(len(numeric_columns)), numeric_columns)

# set and print the plot
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

DCD 2025 dataframe¶
DCD Correlation matrix annotated¶
In [48]:
# Create correlation matrix for dcd25_renamed
dcd25_numeric_columns = dcd25_renamed.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns.tolist()
dcd25_numeric_data = dcd25_renamed[dcd25_numeric_columns]
dcd25_correlation_matrix = dcd25_numeric_data.corr()
In [49]:
# note that the cost difference between requested and actual spending correlates relatively highly with the test score gap 
# this might be useful for further analysis
# for instance, could this be due to the specific district's characteristics?

# also note that the percent of ELL highly correlates to the percent of Hispanic students, this
# might be due to a large number of ELL students being Hispanic and may require further analysis

# Add text annotations and highlight specific cells
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
dcd25_cmap = plt.cm.RdBu_r

im = plt.imshow(dcd25_correlation_matrix, cmap=dcd25_cmap, vmin=-1, vmax=1)
plt.colorbar(im)

# Add text annotations
for i in range(len(dcd25_numeric_columns)):
    for j in range(len(dcd25_numeric_columns)):
        text = plt.text(j, i, f'{dcd25_correlation_matrix.iloc[i, j]:.2f}',
                       ha="center", va="center", color="black", fontsize=8)

# Find and highlight only high POSITIVE correlation points (excluding diagonal)
correlation_threshold = 0.6  # Adjust this value as needed
highlight_points = []
for i in range(len(dcd25_numeric_columns)):
    for j in range(len(dcd25_numeric_columns)):
        # Removed abs() to only highlight positive correlations
        if i != j and dcd25_correlation_matrix.iloc[i, j] > correlation_threshold:
            highlight_points.append((i, j))

# Highlight the high correlation points with rectangles
for row, col in highlight_points:
    rect = plt.Rectangle((col - 0.5, row - 0.5), 1, 1, fill=False, 
                         edgecolor='lime', linewidth=2)
    plt.gca().add_patch(rect)

plt.title('District Cost Database Correlation Matrix', fontsize=14)
plt.xticks(np.arange(len(dcd25_numeric_columns)), dcd25_numeric_columns, rotation=45, ha='right')
plt.yticks(np.arange(len(dcd25_numeric_columns)), dcd25_numeric_columns)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Are the data usable? If not, find some new data!¶

depends on the usage scenario. This seems useful. The DCD data and the SID datasets are both usable with a little cleanup. The SID dataset is a little heavy on the features, but doable. None of the dataseets I have seen so far have a target already defined.

Do you need to modify or correct the data in some way?¶

Corrected it by removing some columns and recoding some others

Is there any class imbalance? (Categories that have many more items than other categories).¶

Almost all categories have more unique values than other columns. for instance region4 has 4 unique values while region9 has 9 unique vbaluees, and stabbr has over 50 unique values.

encode categorical data using one-hat encoding¶

In [50]:
dfnew_encoded = pd.get_dummies(dfnew, drop_first=True)
dfnew_encoded.sample(10)

10 rows × 12375 columns

4. Storytelling With Data graph¶

Just like last week: choose any graph in the Introduction of Storytelling With Data. Use matplotlib to reproduce it in a rough way. I don't expect you to spend an enormous amount of time on this; I understand that you likely will not have time to re-create every feature of the graph. However, if you're excited about learning to use matplotlib, this is a good way to do that. You don't have to duplicate the exact values on the graph; just the same rough shape will be enough. If you don't feel comfortable using matplotlib yet, do the best you can and write down what you tried or what Google searches you did to find the answers.

Create the data in python from excel¶

In [52]:
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'Month': ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun', 'Jul', 'Aug', 'Sep', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec'],
    'Ticket Volume Received': [160, 184, 241, 149, 180, 161, 132, 202, 160, 139, 149, 177],
    'Ticket Volume Processed': [160, 184, 237, 148, 181, 150, 123, 156, 126, 104, 124, 140]
}

df_STD_week2 = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df_STD_week2)

   Month  Ticket Volume Received  Ticket Volume Processed
0    Jan                     160                      160
1    Feb                     184                      184
2    Mar                     241                      237
3    Apr                     149                      148
4    May                     180                      181
5    Jun                     161                      150
6    Jul                     132                      123
7    Aug                     202                      156
8    Sep                     160                      126
9    Oct                     139                      104
10   Nov                     149                      124
11   Dec                     177                      140

create similar plot¶

In [ ]:
# Line chart comparing received vs processed ticket volumes
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

months = df_STD_week2['Month']
received = df_STD_week2['Ticket Volume Received']
processed = df_STD_week2['Ticket Volume Processed']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.plot(months, received, color='grey', label='Received', linewidth=3)
ax.plot(months, processed, color='blue', label='Processed', linewidth=3)
ax.set_title('Ticket Volume Over Time')
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Ticket Count')
ax.set_ylim(0, 300)
ax.grid(False)

# Put labels at the end of each line
ax.annotate("Received", xy=(months.iloc[-1], received.iloc[-1]),
            xytext=(6, 0), textcoords='offset points', va='center', color='grey', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
ax.annotate("Processed", xy=(months.iloc[-1], processed.iloc[-1]),
            xytext=(6, 0), textcoords='offset points', va='center', color='blue', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')

# Text to display at the top
top_text = 'Please approve the hire of 2 FTEs'
top_text2 = 'to backfill those who quit in the past year.'
explanation_text = '2 employees quit in May.'
explanation_text2 = 'We nearly kept up with incoming volume'
explanation_text3 = 'in the following 2 months, but fell behind with the increase in August and'
explanation_text4 = 'haven\'t been able to catch up since.'

# Annotations explaining the trend
ax.text(0.2, 0.95, explanation_text, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=11,
         verticalalignment='top', weight='bold', color='grey')
ax.text(0.53, 0.95, explanation_text2, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=10,
         verticalalignment='top', color='grey')
ax.text(0.2, 0.88, explanation_text3, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=10,
         verticalalignment='top', color='grey')
ax.text(0.25, 0.82, explanation_text4, transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=10,
         verticalalignment='top', color='grey')

# Annotations at specific points
ax.annotate(f"{received.iloc[11]}", xy=(months.iloc[11], received.iloc[11]),
            xytext=(0, 10), textcoords='offset points', ha='center', fontsize=9)
ax.annotate(f"{processed.iloc[11]}", xy=(months.iloc[11], processed.iloc[11]),
            xytext=(0, 10), textcoords='offset points', ha='center', fontsize=9, color='blue')

# Add dots at the end of each line
ax.plot(months.iloc[-1], received.iloc[-1], 'o', color='grey', markersize=8)
ax.plot(months.iloc[-1], processed.iloc[-1], 'o', color='blue', markersize=8)

# Highlight period of disruption
disruption_start = 4  # May (index 4)
ax.axvline(x=disruption_start, color='grey', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

# Shaded area where differences occur
ax.axvspan(4, 11, alpha=0.1, color='grey', label='dropping volume period')

# Text at the top
ax.text(9, 380, top_text,fontsize=24, ha='right', va='top', color='grey', weight='bold')
ax.text(5, 345, top_text2,fontsize=14, ha='right', va='top', color='grey')

# ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('STD2_ticket_volume_comparison.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
plt.close()
